draft.introduction: Book 10 of Eusebius’s Church History celebrates the end of persecution and Constantine’s victory, culminating in the Edict of Milan (313 CE). Eusebius presents this as the providential fulfillment of the church’s long struggle.

translation.text: Thus by the express appointment of God, two roots of goodness, Constantine and Licinius, having driven every tyrant away, acknowledged the benefits which God had conferred upon them. Both with one will and purpose pronounced a law most just and in full sight of all established peace. All men were freed from the oppression of the tyrants, and the former gloomy days gave way to serene brightness.

translator_notes.note: Eusebius’s triumphalist narrative of Constantine’s victory as divine providence established the template for Christian political theology—the idea that God works through emperors to advance the church. This “Eusebian” model was challenged from the start (Donatists rejected imperial involvement) and remains controversial, but it shaped the relationship between church and state for over a millennium.
